clean_story:
Nature’s tiny scissors. Breaking waste down fast.

The word enzyme comes from Greek enzymos—“in yeast”—because the first ones were found in fermenting beer. But they’re everywhere: microscopic machines that chop, twist, and rearrange molecules like a locksmith picking apart a stubborn padlock.

Plastic recycling without enzymes is like untangling headphones with oven mitts—slow, clumsy, and often useless. Enzymes work differently. Take PETase, discovered in 2016 in a Japanese trash dump. This enzyme snips plastic bottles (PET) into raw bits in days, not centuries. Another, LCC, tackles mixed fabrics—like separating cotton from polyester in old T-shirts—by dissolving the stitches between fibers. No heat, no toxic chemicals. Just precision cuts, like a surgeon removing stitches.

Factories now use enzyme “cocktails” to recycle 90% of a plastic bottle into new bottles—something mechanical recycling can’t do. The catch? Enzymes are picky. PETase ignores nylon; LCC won’t touch rubber. Scientists are now engineering “super-enzymes” that work faster and on more materials, like a Swiss Army knife for waste.

Three related subjects are fungi eating plastic, bacteria turning waste to fuel, and how spiders recycle silk.
